title: Quiz: Hash-Based Signature Implementation
description: Test your understanding of implementation considerations and performance optimization

questions.question: Why is constant-time implementation important for hash-based signatures?

options.text: To prevent timing side-channel attacks that leak secret information

questions.explanation: Constant-time implementation ensures execution time doesn't depend on secret values. Without this, attackers can measure timing differences to learn secret keys, even if the cryptographic math is secure.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of BDS (Buchmann-Dahmen-Schneider) tree traversal?

options.text: O(h) space instead of O(2^h) for the full tree

questions.explanation: BDS traversal computes authentication paths efficiently without storing the entire Merkle tree. It achieves O(h) space complexity and O(h/2) average hash operations per signature, making large trees practical.

questions.question: In WOTS+ with parameter w, what is the tradeoff as w increases?

options.text: Larger signatures but fewer hash operations
Smaller signatures but more hash operations per signature
Faster key generation but slower verification
Better security but larger public keys

questions.explanation: Higher w means fewer chains (smaller signature) but each chain has up to w-1 hash operations. For example, w=256 has fewer signature elements than w=16, but requires more hashing.

questions.question: Which hash function variant is generally fastest on 64-bit platforms?
questions.explanation: SHA-512 uses 64-bit operations internally, making it faster on 64-bit processors than SHA-256 (which uses 32-bit operations). This is why some schemes offer SHA-512 variants.

questions.question: Why is SPHINCS+-128f faster than SPHINCS+-128s despite larger signatures?

options.text: It uses a faster hash function
It has more hypertree layers, reducing computation per layer

questions.explanation: The 'f' (fast) variant uses more hypertree layers (d=22 vs d=7 for 128s), making each layer's tree shorter. This trades larger signatures for fewer hash operations during signing.

questions.question: For firmware signing on an HSM, which scheme is most appropriate?

options.text: XMSS or LMS (stateful, HSM manages state)

questions.explanation: HSMs can securely manage state, making stateful schemes like XMSS and LMS ideal. They offer smaller signatures (~2.5KB vs ~8KB+) and the HSM prevents dangerous state reuse.

questions.question: What makes constant-time byte comparison different from regular comparison?

options.text: It always examines all bytes instead of short-circuiting on first difference

questions.explanation: Regular == comparison stops at the first differing byte (short-circuits). Constant-time comparison examines all bytes regardless of content, ensuring timing doesn't reveal where differences occur.

questions.question: For a hybrid PQC deployment, what combination provides defense in depth?
questions.explanation: Combining SPHINCS+ (hash-based, minimal assumptions) with Dilithium (lattice-based, smaller/faster) provides defense in depth. If one's security assumptions fail, the other may still hold.

questions.question: SPHINCS+ signing requires more hash operations than Dilithium signing.
questions.explanation: True! SPHINCS+ requires hundreds to thousands of hash operations per signature (FORS + hypertree layers). Dilithium uses polynomial arithmetic and is typically 100-1000× faster for signing.
